1.synthesis:
Deuteronomy 24:1–4 legislates a specific and limited case in divorce law — not an authorization of divorce but a regulation of remarriage after divorce. The situation is: a man marries a woman, divorces her by writing her a certificate of divorce, and she remarries another man who also divorces her or dies. In that case, her first husband may not remarry her — “she has been defiled” (v. 4). The prohibition prevents the use of the divorce certificate as a mechanism for cycling a woman in and out of marriage for the first husband's convenience. The certificate of divorce (sefer keritut — “scroll of cutting off”) protects the woman precisely because it gives her legal freedom to remarry: it establishes that the first marriage is over and the woman is not a bigamist if she marries again.

The immediately following law (v. 5) is an exemption from military service and all public duties for a newly married man for one year: “He shall be free at home one year to be happy with his wife whom he has taken.” The newlywed exemption is the positive counterpart to the divorce-remarriage restriction: Deuteronomy protects both the beginning of marriage (no military conscription in year one) and the integrity of marriage after divorce (no remarriage to the first husband after the woman has been with another). Together they establish the covenant community's commitment to the dignity and integrity of the marriage relationship as a covenant institution, not merely a contractual arrangement to be manipulated for personal advantage.
1.historical_context: The certificate of divorce (v. 1) is the first appearance of a formal divorce document in the Torah. Its precise form is not specified here; later Jewish tradition developed the get as the standard divorce document. The “something indecent” (ervat davar, literally “nakedness of a thing”) as the grounds for divorce in v. 1 became the subject of famous rabbinic debate between the schools of Shammai (restricted to sexual immorality) and Hillel (any displeasure). Jesus engages this debate directly in Matthew 19:3–9, citing Deuteronomy 24:1 as a Mosaic concession to human hardness of heart rather than God's creation intention. The newlywed military exemption (v. 5) parallels the other personal-completion exemptions of Deuteronomy 20:5–7.
1.literary_structure: Deuteronomy 24:1–5 contains two related but distinct units: the divorce-remarriage prohibition (vv. 1–4) and the newlywed exemption (v. 5). The first is primarily a protective regulation for divorced women; the second is a protective provision for new marriages. Both frame the marriage relationship as deserving legal protection that ordinary social and military demands must respect. The prohibition on defiling the land through wrongful remarriage (v. 4: “you shall not bring sin upon the land”) connects sexual covenant integrity to land covenant integrity — consistent with the Deuteronomic theology that the land is YHWH's and is affected by the covenant behavior of its inhabitants.
1.theological_application: Jesus' engagement with the divorce question in Matthew 19:3–9 reveals the Deuteronomic divorce regulation's proper status: it is a concession to human hardness of heart, not the Creator's intention for marriage. “From the beginning it was not so” — the creation norm of Genesis 2:24 (one flesh) precedes and supersedes the Deuteronomic concession. Deuteronomy 24:1–4 does not celebrate divorce but limits its damage: it prevents the most exploitative use of the divorce certificate (recycling a woman between marriages for the convenience of the first husband). The newlywed exemption's provision that a new husband may “be happy with his wife” (v. 5) is the covenant's positive vision for marriage: a full year of undivided commitment, before the demands of civic life impinge.
1.christology: The marriage covenant protected by Deuteronomy 24:1–5 is the type of the covenant between Christ and the church, explored most fully in Ephesians 5:25–32. Paul's application of Genesis 2:24 to Christ and the church — the two becoming one flesh as a “profound mystery” — is the christological fulfillment of the marriage covenant's creation design. The Deuteronomic protection of the marriage relationship from exploitation corresponds to Christ's permanent commitment to the church: the one who has given himself for her “to sanctify her” does not retain the right to “send her away.” The newlywed exemption's vision of a year of happiness free from external demands images the eschatological feast — the time of undivided rejoicing in the covenant relationship that awaits the church as bride when all competing demands cease.

6.synthesis:
Deuteronomy 24:6–22 is one of the richest concentrations of social justice legislation in the entire Torah, covering ten distinct protections for the economically and socially vulnerable: the prohibition on taking a millstone as a pledge (v. 6), the law against kidnapping fellow Israelites (v. 7), the leprosy reminder (vv. 8–9), pledge regulations protecting the poor (vv. 10–13), the daily wage law (vv. 14–15), the principle of individual rather than inherited guilt (v. 16), the protection of sojourner and widow in litigation (v. 17), and three gleaning laws for the harvest (vv. 19–22). The unifying principle is stated at vv. 17–18: “You shall not pervert the justice due to the sojourner or to the fatherless, or take a widow's pledge... you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you from there.”

The pledge regulations are especially striking: a creditor must not enter the debtor's house to take his pledge but must wait outside for the debtor to bring it out (vv. 10–11); if the debtor is poor, the creditor must return the cloak pledged before nightfall because it is his only covering for sleep (vv. 12–13). The daily wage law requires that a hired worker be paid each day before sunset: “for he is poor and counts on it. Otherwise he may cry to the LORD against you, and you would be guilty of sin” (v. 15). The three gleaning laws — for the forgotten sheaf (v. 19), the olive tree beaten a second time (v. 20), and the vineyard gleaned again (v. 21) — all specify that the residual harvest belongs to the sojourner, orphan, and widow. The harvest is not simply the landowner's property to maximize; it is a covenant resource in which the landless have a stake.
6.historical_context: The millstone prohibition (v. 6) protects the subsistence tool: taking both grinding stones as a pledge would be “taking a life in pledge,” because the millstone was the equipment for daily food preparation. The kidnapping prohibition (v. 7) carries the death penalty — the strongest possible statement that a person's freedom is not a commodity. The individual guilt principle (v. 16) — “Fathers shall not be put to death because of their children, nor shall children be put to death because of their fathers. Each one shall be put to death for his own sin” — is cited in 2 Kings 14:6 as the basis for Amaziah's decision not to execute the children of his father's murderers, and is the basis for Ezekiel's extensive treatment of individual accountability in Ezekiel 18. The gleaning laws are implemented narratively in Ruth 2, where Boaz enforces the gleaning rights of the Moabite widow Ruth.
6.literary_structure: Deuteronomy 24:6–22 is a thematically unified but formally diverse collection. The motivational formula appears three times: Exodus memory (v. 18), Exodus memory again (v. 22), and the threat of divine attention to the cry of the mistreated laborer (v. 15). All three motivational grounds are rooted in YHWH's character as the God who hears the cry of the poor (Exodus) and continues to hear it (v. 15's implication). The gleaning laws (vv. 19–22) close the section with the most concrete application of the section's principle: literal agricultural provision for the landless, built into the harvest process itself.
6.theological_application: The Exodus memory as the ground of economic justice (v. 18, v. 22) establishes a theological chain: YHWH delivered Israel from economic exploitation; therefore Israel must not exploit those vulnerable within its covenant community. The chain is not abstract moral reasoning but concrete theological identity: you were the poor day laborer; you were the sojourner without land rights; you know what it is to be powerless before a creditor. The daily wage law's specific language — “he is poor and counts on it” (v. 15) — personalizes the economic obligation: there is a person on the other end of every labor relationship, and his survival depends on the covenant's protection of his wages. James 5:4 explicitly echoes this law in its condemnation of wealthy landowners who withhold wages.
6.christology: The gleaning laws — leaving harvest residuals for the sojourner, orphan, and widow — are fulfilled in Christ's own ministry to those outside the mainstream of covenant blessing, and in his reversal of the gleaning dynamic: instead of leaving residuals for the poor, he provides superabundance for them. At the feeding of the five thousand, twelve baskets of fragments remain after feeding the crowd from five loaves and two fish (Matthew 14:20) — the fragments themselves exceed what gleaning could have provided. The individual guilt principle of v. 16 — each one dies for his own sin — is the anthropological premise that Paul resolves christologically: in Adam all die (each person's individual participation in Adam's failure), but in Christ all are made alive (1 Corinthians 15:22) — not by corporate inheritance but by individual union with the one who bore each person's individual guilt.
